coredata.dc:title: Recyclable waste Dry-cell batteries derived carbon dots (CDs) for detection of Two-fold metal ions and degradation of BTB dye
coredata.dc:creator: Annamalai, Kumaresan
Annamalai, Arun
Ravichandran, Ramya
Elumalai, Sundaravadivel
coredata.dc:description: In modern era, electronic wastes are one of the major threats around us, most of them are reused with less efficiency instead of re-usage, and conversion into valuable products is highly recommended. In this work, we report an innovative approach for the synthesis of highly photoluminescent CDs from waste dry-cell batteries through one-step hydrothermal treatment for the detection and degradation of environmental pollutants. The as-prepared CDs were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), HR-TEM studies, X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectrometer, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–visible spectrophotometer, and spectrofluorometric measurements. The calculated quantum yield for synthesized CDs was around 13 %. The CDs have uniform particle size distribution, strong photoluminescent behavior, and possess high stability against various environmental conditions. Also, CDs display the selective and sensitive detection of Cr6+ and Co2+ and ions with a detection limit of around 0.11 µM and 0.10 µM respectively. The possible mechanism of CDs was also examined. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of CDs with Bromothymol Blue (BTB) dye was studied. The degradation efficiency of BTB dye can be achieved at around 84 % over 180 min under the irradiation of direct sunlight in presence of H2O2. To date, it’s the first time we have recycled waste dry-cell batteries into CDs as an effective probe for the detection and decomposition of environmental pollution. Furthermore, this work provides not only an easier route to make good quality and improved photoluminescent CDs from waste material like used batteries and also paves way for the reconversion of global treating waste. Finally, the outstanding detection capability with multiple properties of as-prepared CDs provides various environmental applications like the detection of pollutants and carcinogenic polluted water treatment.

head.ce:title: Recyclable waste Dry-cell batteries derived carbon dots (CDs) for detection of Two-fold metal ions and degradation of BTB dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In modern era, electronic wastes are one of the major threats around us, most of them are reused with less efficiency instead of re-usage, and conversion into valuable products is highly recommended. In this work, we report an innovative approach for the synthesis of highly photoluminescent CDs from waste dry-cell batteries through one-step hydrothermal treatment for the detection and degradation of environmental pollutants. The as-prepared CDs were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), HR-TEM studies, X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectrometer, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–visible spectrophotometer, and spectrofluorometric measurements. The calculated quantum yield for synthesized CDs was around 13 %. The CDs have uniform particle size distribution, strong photoluminescent behavior, and possess high stability against various environmental conditions. Also, CDs display the selective and sensitive detection of Cr6+ and Co2+ and ions with a detection limit of around 0.11 µM and 0.10 µM respectively. The possible mechanism of CDs was also examined. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of CDs with Bromothymol Blue (BTB) dye was studied. The degradation efficiency of BTB dye can be achieved at around 84 % over 180 min under the irradiation of direct sunlight in presence of H2O2. To date, it’s the first time we have recycled waste dry-cell batteries into CDs as an effective probe for the detection and decomposition of environmental pollution. Furthermore, this work provides not only an easier route to make good quality and improved photoluminescent CDs from waste material like used batteries and also paves way for the reconversion of global treating waste. Finally, the outstanding detection capability with multiple properties of as-prepared CDs provides various environmental applications like the detection of pollutants and carcinogenic polluted water treatment.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Waste dry-cell batteries derived carbon dots (CDs)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern times, energy storage is a fascinating matter owing to the requirement of energy in the contemporary lifecycle but the wastage from energy storage materials is polluting the globe. To safeguard the world, it is essential to reprocess electronic waste materials for ecological growth in the future (Cheng et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019). Currently, a vast amount of discarded waste dry-cell batteries from every emerging electronics industry is an immense ecological problem. The oldest dry cell battery is still used universally in the TV remote, AC remote controls, and clocks. In waste dry-cell batteries, not all the elements are ecologically friendly, so an appropriate technique is required to recycle them. Every year, more than 1.9 million tons of discarded batteries are produced nevertheless merely 48% is reprocessed. The heavy metals present inside the dry-cell batteries after prolonged use, mix up with the soil and have detrimental effects on human well-being in addition to the environment and is due to the lack of reprocessing guidelines in most of the global nations. Waste batteries are deposited into the atmosphere, creating land and environmental pollution due to the presence of toxic metals like Lead, Zinc which causes acute diseases in humans (Ferella, De Michelis, et al., 2008).

Presently scientists focus on recycling carbon sources from waste materials, as they are abundant, economical, and ecologically friendly (Liu et al., 2019). Among the carbon material members, carbon dots (CDs) are one of the recently emerging nanomaterials with below 10 nm in size and are named carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (A. Annamalai et al., 2022b). In recent years massive responses have been drawn towards CDs owing to their distinctive photo and physicochemical properties such as steady photoluminescence, less toxicity, high water solubility, surface modification, excitation wavelength-dependent emission, and photo bleaching resistivity (He et al., 2021). In terms of solubility, photo stability, and surface functionalization, CDs are superior when related to fluorescent conventional dyes (A. Annamalai et al., 2022a). Due to these properties, CDs generate an appropriate nanomaterial used in widespread applications such as photo catalysts (Chu et al., 2019), bio-imaging sensors, and drug delivery (P. Das et al., 2019) applications. Furthermore, it has been concluded that several types of materials and techniques were used to synthesize carbon quantum dots. These techniques include electrochemical carbonization, hydrothermal treatment (Jana et al., 2019), laser ablation, and microwave-assisted methods (Qi et al., 2019), and the materials include toxic chemicals, bio-mass, and natural products. Amongst these techniques, strong acid conditions are required for carbonization, and the microwave irradiation process is inadequate due to irregular and uncontrolled reaction conditions (Li et al., 2014).

In recent years, hydrothermal treatment exhibits low cost, eco-friendly, and very easy mode to make unique CDs from numerous precursors without the employment of any harmful chemical solvent (Hong et al., 2019). Due to the toxic nature and inadequate amounts of organic precursors (such as glycerol, polybasic acid, and chitosan) used for the preparation of CDs paves a way for a researcher to find an alternate route for this issue (Alam et al., 2015). Despite the above reason, for bulky synthesis and extensive applications, a natural precursor is a better idea, i.e., a greener method can be utilized. The carbon precursors were found to be more abundant in nature, which offers cost-effective, and enhanced optical properties providing freedom for the researcher to work on innovative ideas, and there are several ways to improve superior stable quantum dots. To synthesize the CDs, there were enormous varieties of natural precursors available such as radish, ginger, grass, orange, milk, apple, sugarcane juice, beetroot, carrot, and plant leaves, etc., (Meng et al., 2019). Improving CDs with stable photoluminescent (PL) properties and high quantum yield (QY) via a greener facile method is a vital task. Nowadays researchers giving more attention to synthesizing nitrogen-doped CDs because nitrogen doping can enhance the electronic, and surface properties and chemical reactivity due to the availability of five valence electrons in the.
N-atom that can form bonds with the C-atom. Some of the naturally occurring precursors possess several surface groups which make the CDs stable to improve the optical properties (Meng et al., 2019).

Extensive industrial development and factory discharges are triggering enormous heavy metal pollution and making consequent damage to the aquatic environment. Among the numerous heavy metals, especially hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) and divalent cobalt (Co2+) are relatively harmful when it reaches an excess level in the environment, especially around industrial zones because industrial effluents are one of the major sources of waste which contains hexavalent chromium ions is lethal to animals and humans (Ying et al., 2019), (Elabbas et al., 2016). Hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) ion is one of the common carcinogenic toxic metals released into the atmosphere predominantly via steel industries, leather tanning industries, etc., causing dermatitis, ulcers, lung and liver cancers, tumors, etc., (Fontaine et al., 2019). According to WHO (World Health Organization), the acceptable limit for Cr6+ in drinking water is 0.05 mg/L. Thus, it is mandatory to detect Cr6+ in drinking water in the environment. Divalent cobalt ion (Co2+) is an essential metal ion in the human biological system in trace amounts, with major roles as a constituent of cobalt-containing co-enzymes and Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) (Lison, 2021). The uptake of cobalt usually happens in our day-to-day life through food consumption or drinking of water. The concentration of cobalt ions in most drinking water is lesser than 1–2 ppb (parts per billion). While exceeding the minimum uptake of cobalt is related to several health concerns like thyroid problems, asthma, etc., (Gharehbaghi & Shemirani, 2012). Therefore, it is very essential to monitor the concentration of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions in an aqueous environment. Consequently, it is necessary to design selective and sensitive methods for the detection of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions. To investigate the Cr6+ and Co2+ ions in drinking water, various methods have been reported such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), electrochemical methods, ICP-AES, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods, chemiluminescence, and fluorescence spectroscopy methods have been used for detection of metal ions (Karimi et al., 2011; Scheel & Tarley, 2017). Among the above techniques, fluorometry has fascinated much more attention for sensing metals due to its high sensitivity, low-cost detection, rapid response, and simple approach. Recently over the last few years, various kinds of fluorescent probes have been reported for the detection of Co2+ ions (Jing et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2016). The improvement of a simplistic, prompt, and sensitive approach for the trace recognition of Cr6+ is in extremely demanded.

All over the world, water pollution is one of the major serious issues for human health and environmental threat (Angel Ezhilarasi et al., 2018). The major water pollutants are organic dyes, and several industrial effluents like plastic, textile, leather, etc., are considered to be a liquid waste contaminant (Sreekanth et al., 2017). The organic dye materials are carcinogenic, non-biodegradable, toxic, and chemically stable, thus polluting the environment and creating a potential barrier to light penetration of aquatic systems (T. Das et al., 2019). Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation of organic dye materials has been followed by several physical and chemical methods. The methods such as membrane filtration, Ultraviolet radiation, ion-exchange, adsorption, H2O2 oxidations, and chemical coagulation but does not effectively degrade the organic dyes (Chaleshtori et al., 2013). For the effective degradation of organic dye molecules, the photocatalytic method plays a vital role. The dye used in this study was a triphenylmethane dye Bromothymol blue (BTB), an anionic dye. BTB is one of the sulfone-phthalein dyes, which is often used as a colored indicator of pH and is an unhindered textile dye derivative. Bromothymol blue has halochromic properties and does not undergo oxidation directly due to its viable visible absorbance range at 400 to 700 nm. Therefore, it serves as a model organic dye for free radicals via photocatalytic degradation (Prakash et al., 2018).

To date, we have turned, used/waste batteries into CDs as an effective probe for the detection and decomposition of environmental pollutants. Here we reported the synthesis of waste dry-cell batteries derived carbon dots (CDs), a greener method for metal ion sensing and degradation of BTB dye. To synthesize the nitrogen-doped CDs with carbon rods under hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C. Primarily photo-sensitive and photocatalytic properties were examined. The blue emissive CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission, with an average diameter of 4.07 nm, and are used as a probe for the two-fold detection of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions independently through inner-filter effect (IFE) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. Furthermore, CDs additionally aided in the degradation of BTB dye with H2O2, under the irradiation of sunlight. The degradation efficiency of 84 % can be achieved with the rate constant value of 9.95 × 10-3 min−1. Our research findings CDs provide better detection and degradation efficiency against environmental pollutants in an aqueous medium and it would be considered a better environment-friendly pollutant remediation candidate.

Carbon rod from waste zinc-carbon dry-cell batteries, ethylene diamine (99%), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (99%), SnCl4 (99.9%), Pb(NO3)2 (98%), NiCl2·6H2O (98%), CrCl3·6H2O (98%), Hg(CH3COO)2 (98%), Anhydrous CdCl2 (99%), FeCl3·6H2O (97%), MnCl2·4H2O (97%), CuCl2·2H2O, NaCl (99.9%), CoCl2·6H2O (98%), CaCl2 (94–95%) and K2Cr2O7 (99.9%) was purchased SRL private limited·H2O2 (30% w/v extra pure) purchased from TCI chemicals limited. All the chemicals are used here without any additional purification. Milli-Q water was used for the whole experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of carbon powder from waste Dry-cell batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon rod was taken from the waste Zinc-Carbon dry-cell batteries from the garbage. Initially, we removed the carbon rod from the waste batteries and washed five times with Milli-Q water to remove the unwanted particles like metals, MnO2 and carbon. Then dried at room temperature for 24 h and crushed with mortar to get the fine carbon powder. Furthermore, carbon powder is treated with aquaregia (3:1 ratio of HCl + HNO3) and heated for 24 h because in order to remove the inorganic materials present in it. At last, the carbon powder was centrifuged and washed with Milli-Q water until it becomes to neutral pH and it is dried in oven for 24 h at 60 °C.

The freshly prepared fine product of 100 mg carbon powder treated with 5 % ethylene diamine solution under sonication for 10 min. After sonication, the reactant solution mixture was then transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave under hydrothermal treatment kept at 180 °C for 6 h (Kapitonov et al., 2018). The as-synthesized CDs were cooled down to room temperature. After the cooling process, the product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm and then filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane used for purification to eliminate the unreacted larger constituents. The CDs solutions were further dialyzed by using a dialysis membrane bag against Milli-Q water with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da for 48 h. The as-purified CDs were used for further characterization studies and the synthesis procedure was represented in Fig. 1 .

The morphology and structure of the samples were examined with High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL JEM − 2100 Plus HRTEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by PANanalytical Aeris Benchtop X-ray diffractogram equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) as an X-ray source. The surface properties were examined with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ULVAC-PHI 5000 probe III XPS). The Zeta potential measurements were carried out with HORIBA Particle Size Analyzer SZ-100. FTIR spectra of CDs were measured with Shimadzu IR Tracer 100 with ATR mode. The Photoluminescence (PL) emission measurements were carried out using a HORIBA Fluoromax R928P and the fluorescence lifetime decay was recorded by Time-Resolved Photoluminescence Spectrometer (FLS 1000). The absorption spectrum was recorded with the help of a Carry-60 UV–Visible spectrometer.

The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated at around 13 % by using quinine sulfate (QS) as the reference standard. For the calculation of quantum yield, five concentrations were made, all of which had absorbance less than 0.1. Quinine Sulfate (literature ɸ = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index (η) of 1.33) while the CDs were dissolved in water (η = 1.33). Their fluorescence spectrum was recorded at the excitation of 366 nm for QS, then by comparing the integrated PL intensities excited at 350 nm of the CDs were determined. The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated by using equation (1),
(1)

where, QRef is the quantum yield of reference (quinine sulfate), IASam and IARef are the integral areas of reference and sample, ODRef and ODSam are the optical density of reference and sample,

is the refractive index of reference and sample, respectively (Liang et al., 2016). All the measurements were taken by using Carry-60 UV–Visible spectrometer (optical density) and HORIBA Fluoromax R928P spectrometer (integral area).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for recognition of dual metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the selectivity of CDs for Co2+ and Cr6+ sensors, the quenching effects of different positive metal ions such as Zn2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+ measurements were examined. A sequence of prepared 1 mmol stock solution of various metal ions was added individually to the CDs probe solution, corresponding emission spectra were recorded at 428 nm. Using the fluorescence spectroscopic technique, the ability of the fluorescence response of as-prepared CDs against several metal ions was examined. All the metal ion solutions were prepared from their corresponding metal salts. Primarily, a concentration of 1 mM stock solution of metal ions was made into 10 ml using Milli-Q water. For the detection of metal ions, 100 µL of CDs in 2.5 ml of Milli-Q water was taken in a quartz cuvette as a probe solution, for measurements under room temperature conditions. A known concentration of several metal ions solution was individually added to the probe solutions. After the addition of several metal ions to the fluorescence probe solution, especially the fluorescence intensity of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions were quenched. The intensity of the fluorescence emission was measured at 428 nm with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, instantly after the addition of CDs solution without any post-action. For the selectivity study, the effect of interference from several metal ions, and the known concentrations of other toxic metal ions was added to the CDs probe solution individually. Under similar experimental conditions, their corresponding emission spectrum was recorded. A bar diagram for the fluorescence quenching was plotted against several metal ions versus intensity repeated 3 times. For a better comparison of CD's selectivity, a bar graph was plotted as an error bar shown in Fig. S2 a) and b).

The photocatalytic degradation activity of CDs against Bromothymol blue (BTB) dye was studied under the irradiation of sunlight. The photocatalytic degradation of BTB dye was carried out in 50 ml of 10 ppm dye solution with 2 ml of H2O2 using the 3 ml of CDs as a catalyst under magnetic stirring. Under the sunlight, the whole solution was kept in an open atmosphere for irradiation, and the sample was collected from the reaction solution mixture at regular time intervals of 30 min. With the help of the absorption spectrum, the degradation efficiency of the collected samples was measured by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Fig. 2 a displays the FE-SEM image of a finely powdered carbon rod which looks flake-like large structure of about 3.93 ± 1 µm, followed by Fig. 2b TEM images of CDs, clearly indicating that CDs are quasi-spherical, and are well dispersed in nature. The as-prepared CDs have an average size distribution centered at 4.07 nm inset in Fig. 2b. In Fig. 2c shows the characteristic HR-TEM images of CDs, representing a space lattice of 0.36 nm, and are in good agreement with the [002] planes of graphite rod (Wu et al., 2014), demonstrated in the XRD patterns. Fig. 2d corresponds to the elemental composition of CDs as weight percentage is 65.51 % of C, 27.05 % of N, and 7.44 % of O respectively. Therefore, the EDS spectrum shows that as-prepared CDs are free from other metals present in the battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, FTIR, and Raman analysis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray diffraction pattern is a potent tool for determining the structure of CDs. Fig. 3 a. shows the XRD pattern of the used battery rod (bare material-carbon rod) and CDs. From the XRD pattern of the used battery rod, the highest sharp peak at 2θ of about 25.15° was attributed to the (002) plane and the small peak at 43.5° was assigned to the (001) plane (Xie et al., 2016), which matches to graphitic carbon and a disorderly structural arrangement. Furthermore, the highest intensity of the peak at 25.15° designates a high concentration of the crystalline phase in the used battery rod (bare material). The broad peak at 2θ = 25.15° in the XRD pattern of CDs shows the presence of amorphous nature in comparison to bare material (crystalline nature). The peak disappears from its crystalline nature to an amorphous form which confirms the formation of CDs. Moreover, the small peak centered at 2θ = 43.5° in CDs validates the condensation of carbon material.

The FTIR spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 3b. The characteristic IR signal at 1335 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of sp2 C–H (Thulasi et al., 2020). The broad peaks at the 3286 cm−1 range correspond to the H-bonded –OH/N–H functional group. Hence it is clear that cleaving the crystalline linkage generated by the organic oxygen in the battery rod via hydrothermal condition makes the CDs highly water-soluble with an average particle size of 4.07 nm (Kumar Thiyagarajan et al., 2016). The peaks at 1635 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of the C = O functional group, and the IR signal at 1086 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O stretching peak from the primary surface analysis it is confirmed that the CDs were composed with rich in functional groups is one of the major roles for excitation dependent emission behavior.

On the other hand, to investigate the structure of carbon materials, Raman scattering measurements were used. Fig. 3c shows two clear peaks centered at about 1343.9 cm−1 and 1589.2 cm−1, which are assigned to the D band and G band respectively. The D band corresponds to the disordered defective graphite’s termination plane of the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds, while the G band is related to the vibrational mode of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice which is accompanied by the E2g mode of graphite. The ID/IG band relates the relative intensity of the D band and G band which reveals the defective nature of the carbon materials. The ratio of ID/IG of CDs was 0.85, which reflects the high degree of defects formed by nitrogenation processes (Ramanan et al., 2018).

From the XPS analysis, the chemical composition of the CDs was examined. The CDs XPS spectrum primarily displays four distinct peaks positioned at 285.2 eV for C 1 s, 399.4 eV for N 1 s, and 532.9 eV for O 1 s respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 a. In Fig. 4b shows High-resolution deconvolution of C 1 s signals of CDs, the three important peaks positioned at 284.7 eV corresponds to sp2 C = C, 285.4 eV for C-OH, and 287.9 eV for C = O observed (Xue et al., 2012). From N 1 s spectrum Fig. 4c resembles the 399.4 eV mainly ascribed to –NH2 groups, signifying that nitrogen-doping on the CDs mainly existed in the form of amine, and 401.3 eV for quaternary N. For the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4d, the two main peaks centered at 531.2 eV correspond to organic C-O, and 532.2 eV for organic C = O (J. Wu et al., 2020).

Under daylight, the aqueous solution of CDs was fully transparent but under a 365 nm UV lamp CDs solution emits strong blue emission. Fig. 5 a shows the typical absorption spectrum of CDs showed a weak absorption at 264 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* transition, owing to the existence of graphitic carbon structure and the broad shoulder peak at 315 nm attributes to the n-π* transition, which resembles the presence of surface functionalized groups (Wang et al., 2019).

Fig. 5b represents CDs are highly photoluminescent followed by exhibiting excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties. Fluorescence spectra obtained by exciting at the optimum excitation wavelength found that the CDs showed maximum emission at 428 nm by an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases from 240 to 450 nm, the maximum emission peak at 428 nm and the wavelength shift towards a red-shift was clearly shown as normalized fluorescence Fig. 5c. The origin of fluorescent properties of CDs may arise from the several defects existing on the surface of CDs (Selvakumar et al., 2022). The excited state average fluorescence lifetime is 6.93 ns (Fig. 5d) calculated using a bi-exponential fitting equation.

The stability of CDs derived from waste dry-cell batteries was studied against multiple environmental factors like ionic strength (Fig. S1a) with increasing concentration of NaCl followed by photo-bleaching (continuous irradiation of light) shown in Fig. S1b, with increasing irradiation time (under 365 nm UV light). From the results obtained from the above evaluation, one can clearly understand that CDs can withstand their stability up to the concentration of 200 mM without any deviation from its control intensity. On the other hand, continuous photo bleaching also does not affect the emission intensity, making CDs a highly photo-stable material. The storage stability of the as-synthesized CDs was evaluated once for 10 days and the CDs retain their stability for 60 days, were plotted in Fig. S1c. It should be noted that as-prepared CDs solutions are translucent and displays strong fluorescence for two months at ambient temperature in air, signifying good fluorescent stability. In the increasing pH range (Fig. S1d), the PL intensity was gradually raised at lower pH, PL intensity was reduced and increased at higher pH it shows that at higher pH there will be a chance for localization of electron because of electron rich environment on the other hand at lower pH it no free electrons are avail so the intensity was reduced (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2016). (Fig. S1e) shows the zeta potential of CDs, where −50 mV shows that the CDs are highly stable and resemble highly functionalized electron-rich functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of CDs against various metal ions
Selective detection of Cr6+ and Co2+ by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a & d) displayed the quenching efficiency of increasing Cr6+ and Co2+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, with increasing the concentration of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was decreased. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs of other metal ions displayed very little quenching effects. The fluorescence intensity of the Cr6+ and Co2+ systems was significantly quenched, which showed the selective detection of metal ions. Fig. S2b, shows the sensitivity of CDs with Cr6+ and Co2+ ions, in presence of other metal cations except for Cr6+ and Co2+ ions for the same, overall, the sensitivity against other metal cations shows good sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity assay and Stern-Volmer quenching of Co2+ and Cr6+ with CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To calculate the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) value and limit of detection (LOD for sensitivity measurement) of the fluorescent sensor probes, the photoluminescent emission data obtained from changing the concentrations (0–190 µM) of both Cr6+ and Co2+ ions were plotted and fitted against linear Ksv equation, expressed as,
I o
I
= 1 +
K
sv
M
where, Io is the initial fluorescence intensities and I is the after-addition of both quenching ions, [M] denotes the ion concentration and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

The observed results indicated that the fluorescent peaks of CDs with the Cr6+ and Co2+ system had undergone the bathochromic shift towards red wavelength from 428 to 452 nm. The results show that as the concentration of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions increased; the Io/I value displays non-linear relationship in Fig. 6 (b & e). A linear relation of R2 = 0.99311 and R2 = 0.99501 was observed from the linear range of the fluorescence response 0–117 µM and 6.6–32 µM concentration for Cr6+ and Co2+ in CDs respectively represented in Fig. 6 (c & f). The calculated Ksv values in presence of the Cr6+ and Co2+ metal ions were also found that 0.0265 M−1 and 0.0252 M−1 respectively. Based on the 3σ/K equation (Atchudan et al., 2020) (where the σ-standard deviation of CDs and K-slope of the regression equation), the limit of detection for Cr6+ and Co2+ was calculated to be 0.11 µM and 0.10 µM respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) values is compared with other works shown in Table S1 and S2.

The probable mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of CDs derived from waste dry-cell batteries by the two different metal ions was studied. From the results obtained, the characterization techniques like HR-TEM, FT-IR, XPS, and Zeta potential confirms that tiny CDs were composed with functional groups like amine, carbonyl groups, makes CDs as electron-rich species can improves interaction with the positively charged metal cations. In general, the quenching of fluorescence by the metal cations is caused by one of the following mechanisms, are inner-filter effect (IFE), energy transfer process, and Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism (Singaravelu et al., 2021). Initially, the change of absorption range of CDs in presence of both the Cr6+ and Co2+ metal ions individually were studied. Surprisingly, in presence of Cr6+ in CDs aqueous solution shows a red-shift from 315 nm to 370 nm in Fig. S2a which may be due to the CDs forming a complex with the Cr6+, and the whole system becoming complex of CDs-Cr6+ which also reflects in the emission spectrum during the quenching studies. There was a red shift was observed in the emission spectrum after the addition of Cr6+ into probe solution Fig. 6a.

The absorption region of the Cr6+ with CDs and excitation spectrum of CDs are finely overlapped and reabsorbed the excitation range of CDs Fig. 7 a. The average lifetime was unaltered from 6.93 to 7.31 ns in presence of Cr6+ suggested that, the quenching of fluorescence follows the static process and form a stable ground state non-fluorescent complex Fig. 7d. Therefore, the above results are supporting that the quenching of CDs fluorescence over the specific addition of CDs are duly followed inner-filter effect (IFE) mechanism (Zhang et al., 2017). On the other hand, a steady increase in the absorbance band was detected during the addition of Co2+ metal ions into the CDs environment shown in Fig. S2b, Co2+ ions do not lead to any interaction with CDs in the ground state observed. Also, we checked for there is any overlap between the excitation spectrum of CDs with the absorbance of Co2+ metal ions with CDs Fig. 7b, which ruled out the inner-filter effect mechanism. Followed by we have to examine the possibility of FRET (Forster Resonance Energy Transfer) as one of the types of mechanism for quenching process reason for the quenching of fluorescence, for that we have checked for any emission from the Co2+ aqueous solution under the UV light irradiation, unfortunately, there was no emission was observed, so this also ruled out the possibility of FRET mechanism (Bhatt et al., 2020). Therefore, the quenching was observed due to the PET process, the negatively charged CDs coordinate with positively charged metal ions and form complexes (Bhatt et al., 2020), and additionally the unchanged average lifetime of CDs with Co2+ was 6.93 – 7.22 ns. Fig. 7e refers to the turn-off fluorescence attained by the static quenching process and forms a stable ground state non-fluorescence complex, therefore the transfer of electrons after the photoexcitation process from CDs to Co2+ metal ions result in the PET process and the possible detection mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 7c.

Our research findings also validate the applicability of these as-prepared CDs for the efficient photocatalytic decomposition of organic dye materials. Organic dye is a global concern as pollutants from industries are entering into water streams and tragically affecting aquatic species' life.

The photocatalytic activity of CDs was calculated by measuring the decomposition rate of Bromothymol blue (BTB) dye in water under sunlight irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of recyclable CDs was treated for BTB degradation and used as a model pollutant. The degradation of BTB does not undergo direct oxidation degradation. Using H2O2 and CDs, it was degraded chemically via the free radical route. The dye degradation of photocatalyzed CDs was studied by recording the UV–Vis absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 8 a. The maximum intense absorption peak at 616 nm was decreased in the presence of CDs with H2O2 and without H2O2 under the irradiation of sunlight, those were examined in same wavelength of BTB by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 8a shows the carbon dots a metal-free homogeneous catalyst for photocatalytic degradation of Bromothymol blue (BTB) dye. Initially, the catalyst in the BTB dye solution was irradiated under the sunlight for 180 min. When increasing the time of photocatalytic reaction, the absorption peaks show there is not much change in presence of only CDs as a catalyst. But CDs with H2O2, a metal-free catalyst display a decrease in the absorption peaks. The degradation efficiency of BTB dye using CDs with H2O2 was about 84 % can be achieved over the time duration of 180 min but in presence of CDs only it achieved an efficiency of 17 %. Fig. 8d, shows the CDs band gap of 3.43 eV calculated from the Tauc plot (Padmanaban et al., 2021) (equation 1):
(1)

( h υ -
E g
)
n 2
where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck constant, υ is frequency, A is constant, (n = 1) which represents the characteristic of the bandgap for the prepared material (indirect bandgap) and Eg is the energy band gap.

The degradation rate of BTB dye was determined by the following equation (2) (P. Annamalai et al., 2021):
(2)
DegradationRate

where Co is the initial absorption value at time ‘0′, and Ct is the absorption value at a time ‘min’. Under 3 h of sunlight irradiation, almost 84 % of degradation occurs. Fig. 8b shows the plot of C/Co vs time (min). Furthermore, it depicts that, the catalyst with H2O2 displays improved degradation efficiency while related to the catalyst without H2O2. Fig. 8c displays the plot of ln (C/Co) vs time (min) for CDs with H2O2 and without H2O2. The values of the rate constant for CDs with H2O2 and without H2O2 were determined by using the linear plot are 9.95 × 10-3 min−1 and 5.22 x10-4 min−1 respectively and it follows first-order kinetics. From the linear plot obtained for the photocatalytic process, the value of R2 for the degradation of the BTB dye was found to be 0.99 as well as the linear plot confirms the photocatalytic degradation of the dye molecule follows first-order reaction. The above results indicated that CDs with H2O2 have a higher rate of reaction and better degradation. The enhanced efficiency of the comparison of various catalyst along with the rate constant and degradation efficiency for the BTB dye with our as-synthesized waste dry-cell batteries derived CDs as shown in Table S5 (Ayodhya & Veerabhadram, 2019).

The semiconductor-based photocatalysts are excellent candidates for the treatment of contaminated water. Our catalyst produced highly reactive intermediate species like hydroxyl (OH.) and superoxide (O2 .-) radicals (Hoag et al., 2009). In this work, we have studied the photocatalytic ability of CDs for the degradation of Bromothymol blue (BTB) dye in presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). However, the presence of CDs shows a band gap of ∼ 3.43 eV but interestingly the excitation-dependent emission spectrum makes CDs an excellent sunlight conserver. Even though CDs alone can’t able to degrade the BTB dye due to its stable structure, the presence of H2O2 enhances the generation of HO. and also increased the rate of reaction for dye degradation. Under the irradiation of sunlight, the CDs absorb the photons, which are then transferred the electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) and generated the electron-hole pairs on the surface of the photocatalyst (Kumaresan et al., 2022) as shown in Fig. 8e. Whereas in the presence of CDs, H2O2 oxidized on the conduction band (CB) and reduced itself to be highly reactive hydroxyl radical (OH.) species which oxidizes the structure of organic dye Bromothymol blue (BTB) into reactive intermediates, and this practice reduces the rate of recombination process of (e-/h+). To accelerate the degradation of the organic contaminant into minerals such as CO2, H2O, etc., and harmless inorganic acids (Cotto-Maldonado, 2013), accordingly the intermediate species interacted with the photocatalyst’s surface charge.

In this work, H2O2 is supposed to play a significant role in the system, acts as an oxidant, and is frequently used in organic contaminants degradation studies. Here the effect of H2O2 concentration on BTB degradation in CDs/H2O2 systems was explored. Moreover, the catalytic system with a metal-free homogeneous catalyst avoids secondary contamination from the metal ions. Hence, the CDs/H2O2 system displays a great promising candidate in the treatment of organic pollutants cost-effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion about waste Zinc-Carbon Dry-cell batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc–Carbon battery is one of the most economical primary batteries and it hikes consumer’s consumption. In ancient days, the commercialized dry–cell battery is used in possible portable electronic devices. In the core of the dry-cell battery, a graphite rod or carbon rod act as a positive electrode is surrounded by a blend of MnO2 and carbon powder. While the negative part of the electrode is covered by the zinc rod which holds all the components in the container. Annually, a tons of zinc-carbon batteries are used around the world but are not properly recycled. Even though these batteries are harmless in nature, prolonged disposal leads to certain complications to the environment. Owing to passive properties of the carbon rod helps to prevent the electrode to react with the other components in the battery. In the dry-cell battery, the carbon rod and zinc container holds recyclable potential behavior. Hence, we have chosen to recycle the carbon rod as a raw material from the waste dry-cell batteries for the synthesis of cost-effective carbon dots (CDs).

This present research work is highly focused on, not only recycling the highly dumped electronic waste but also finding an alternate track to employ as new material. Conversion of these types of wastes into any other valuable product is not much easy, even if it is recycled can’t give better efficiency (energy storage materials). So, converting into another form is highly recommended but in general, it consumes more manpower and requires high-cost equipment for that we moved to very cost-effective methods toward carbon dots. Because of the easy preparation method and low-cost technique, the waste carbon rods were recycled through the hydrothermal method and utilized as an effective detection probe for carcinogenic metal ion detection and degradation of water-polluting dye. Table S3 represents other waste material’s conversion into carbon dots (CDs). Further developing this methodology for large-scale production will be utilized for the fabrication of portable cost-efficient real-time analyzing sensors for the detection of environmental threats.

We have established a fascinating approach to reconverting the global threat into highly photoluminescence CDs by using a simple hydrothermal treatment for environmental applications. The as-prepared CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission, outstanding photostability with an average particle size of 4.07 nm, PL quantum yield of 13 %, and are used as a fluorescent probe for dual sensing of Cr6+ and Co2+ ions with a sensitivity (LOD) of 0.11 µM and 0.10 µM respectively through the IFE and PET mechanism. The blue emissive CDs show a band gap of ∼ 3.43 eV and excitation wavelength dependent emission behavior additionally aided for the degradation of BTB dye under the irradiation of sunlight. The as-prepared CDs with H2O2 a greener metal-free homogeneous catalyst, with a degradation efficiency of 84 % can be achieved over 180 min with the rate constant value of 9.95 × 10-3 min−1. The fluorescent CDs show very good detection and better degradation efficiency against environmental pollutants in an aqueous medium. The waste dry-cell batteries-derived CDs hold great potential for real-world sensor applications. In the future, CDs will hold a promising candidate for numerous applications such as biological sensing, bio-imaging, and anti-counterfeiting studies.
